title: You Need to Turn This Setting OFF Right Now!

transcript.text: So, YouTube has a setting that's a few years old at this point that's buried deep in the YouTube studio that we've all forgotten about. But, if you have a particular opinion about AI, it suddenly become very, very important. And this is why. It is called Gemini Omni, and it allows you to remix shorts into entirely new videos using prompts. The examples here demonstrate the ability to add a new person into the video, change the appearance, and the backgrounds. Essentially, we're taking all of the components from the original short, including the likeness of any human beings, and creating something that didn't exist before. The expectation YouTube is offering here is the ability to easily step into the trends and conversations of YouTube. But, as we all know, expectation can vary greatly from reality. Allow me to demonstrate. >> Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. Yes. >> So, I made this short a year ago, and I know it's silly, but it's got nearly 3 million views, and it's made it an absolute magnet for remixing. Now, the telltale sign that a short has been remixed are these dots at the bottom of the shorts player, which are the universal design language for swipe left. Okay, then. This is much more intense than just watching a regular gaming video, but the graphics are surprisingly realistic for a blocky world. What? >> OH, >> [screaming] >> I WIN. DID YOU WIN? YES, I did. I won. DID YOU ACTUALLY WIN? >> SO, who is this? What are we doing in my house? And who is holding that bloody phone? >> This is the best moment ever. Let us celebrate with the THE DANCE. >> [screaming] >> NOW, TO BE FAIR, THAT ONE is actually quite accurate. I do the dance like that. >> No way, this is incredible. Look at it go. Woo. Now I'm recording this. >> What did you even prompt to get this? >> [laughter] >> I am really crushing this content creator life today. >> What an absolute jerk. >> As a quick reminder, my original short was remixed by other creators into all of these variants through prompting. Now, I'll leave the opinions on this feature up to you in the comments. Because what's more important to me right now is whether or not you want this happening to your content. Because by default, the permission to do this on your channel and all of your videos is turned on. So, let me show you where to find it. First of all, in the YouTube Studio, if you check out the analytics for a short and go to the engagement tab, you'll find a remix panel, which shows you all of the creators who have remixed your original short. As I said earlier, a lot of people have done just that for this particular short. Now, back on the content management screen in the YouTube Studio, if you go to the video details of a short and scroll all the way down, almost to the very bottom, you'll find this short's remix setting. As you can see, this is set to allow both video and audio remixing. And there is no channel default way to change this. Anyway, to prevent any creators from visually remixing your YouTube shorts, you need to switch this setting to audio only. Now, the key thing to note at this point is if any creators have already remixed your short and you change this setting, then all of those remixes are permanently deleted. So, ultimately, you do have control over whether anybody remixed your shorts if you know where this setting is. And remember to change it every single time you upload a new YouTube short. But, I hear you ask, "What about the hundreds of shorts I've already uploaded to my channel? How do I fix them?" Well, you can do it, but it ain't half clunky. So, on the content screen, there is this column with check boxes where you can select one, many, or all displayed Shorts all at once. Next, click on the edit drop-down here to select Shorts remixing. And that's where you can update the setting for all of these Shorts all at the same time to audio only. There's just one problem with that solution. You can only do 30 Shorts at a time, and I've got 682 of them to do. As far as I'm aware, this reimagining feature for remixing is only available for YouTube Shorts. However, it must also be noted that the remixing setting is available for long-form videos. So, who knows what happens in the future. And having said that, for remixing long-form content, you can turn the entire thing off, but it's defaulted to be completely on. I think what I'm trying to say here is now might be a really good time to order this setting on your entire channel because its ramifications have changed quite significantly recently. And speaking of audits, if you want a super quick one for your entire channel and how to grow faster, then make sure to check out the link in the description. It's completely free, and there's not even any sign-up required. Now, if you're a mobile-only creator, you can check these settings in the YouTube Studio app by tapping the three dots next to a video, going to edit, then swiping down to the community settings, and then finally selecting Shorts remixing. But, I'm going to be honest with you. That's a hell of a lot of effort to check one setting on many videos. Now then, I do have one more critical thing to mention, and it's actually a really good update from YouTube that they've recently introduced. Because these Shorts have essentially been generated by AI tools, they now have an AI label directly on the player to inform viewers of the nature of the content. And it's not just happening here. This update is platform-wide, and it could impact the content you are making. So, join me over here for the full explainer. I'll see you there.
